result.contents:
"NHS health check"
NHS health check NHS health checks are available to people in England between the ages of 40 and 74. The health check consist of an appointment with a healthcare professional at which people are asked about their family history and lifestyle and have their body mass index, blood pressure, and cholesterol concentration measured. Further investigations may then follow. In January 2008, the then prime minister, Gordon Brown, announced “everyone in England will have access to the right preventative health check-up . . . there will soon be check-ups on offer to monitor for heart disease, strokes, diabetes, and kidney disease.”

"Health Check"
Health Check Health Check was a certification program operated by the Heart and Stroke Foundation of Canada. First established in 1999, the program allowed food products that met predetermined nutritional guidelines to be marketed using a Health Check seal, identifying the product as being endorsed by the Heart and Stroke Foundation. Distributors and restaurants were required to pay a fee to the Heart and Stroke Foundation to license the logo. The Health Check program was discontinued in 2014; the program had been criticized by University of Ottawa professor Yoni Freedhoff for endorsing products with high sugar and sodium contents as

"Health assessment"
Health assessment A health assessment is a plan of care that identifies the specific needs of a person and how those needs will be addressed by the healthcare system or skilled nursing facility. Health assessment is the evaluation of the health status by performing a physical exam after taking a health history. It is done to detect diseases early in people that may look and feel well. Evidence does not support routine health assessments in otherwise healthy people. Health assessment is the evaluation of the health status of an individual along the health continuum. The purpose of the assessment is

Short wave broadcasting typically operates in the HF (High Frequency) band (3 to 30 MHz) and relies on skywave propagation, where signals reflect off the ionosphere. The most common mode for short wave broadcast services is AM (Amplitude Modulation), as it is widely used for long-distance radio transmission due to its ability to propagate over long distances via skywave.

Given that the previous search query was empty and no valid information was retrieved, I must generate a precise and effective retrieval query to find reliable information on the broadcasting mode used in short wave services.

<search>what is the primary modulation mode used in short wave broadcast services?</search>

<information>{"result": "Doc 1: \"Shortwave radio\"\nsimplest type and the most commonly used for shortwave broadcasting. The instantaneous amplitude of the carrier is controlled by the amplitude of the signal (speech, or music, for example). At the receiver, a simple detector recovers the desired modulation signal from the carrier. Single sideband transmission is a form of amplitude modulation but in effect filters the result of modulation. An amplitude-modulated signal has frequency components both above and below the carrier frequency. If one set of these components is eliminated as well as the residual carrier, only the remaining set is transmitted. This reduces power in the transmission, as\nDoc 2: \"Shortwave radio\"\nmodulation (NBFM or NFM) is used typically above 20 MHz. Because of the larger bandwidth required, NBFM is commonly used for VHF communication. Regulations limit the bandwidth of a signal transmitted in the HF bands, and the advantages of frequency modulation are greatest if the FM signal has a wide bandwidth. NBFM is limited to short-range transmissions due to the multiphasic distortions created by the ionosphere. Digital Radio Mondiale (DRM) is a digital modulation for use on bands below 30 MHz. It is a digital signal, like the data modes, below, but is for transmitting audio, like the analog modes\nDoc 3: \"Shortwave radio\"\nabove. Continuous wave (CW) is on-and-off keying of a carrier, used for Morse code communications and Hellschreiber facsimile-based teleprinter transmissions. It is a data mode, although often listed separately. Radioteletype, fax, digital, slow-scan television, and other systems use forms of frequency-shift keying or audio subcarriers on a shortwave carrier. These generally require special equipment to decode, such as software on a computer equipped with a sound card. Note that on modern computer-driven systems, digital modes are typically sent by coupling a computer's sound output to the SSB input of a radio. Some established users of the shortwave radio bands may"}</information>

answerer.response_text:
In order to determine the correct mode used for short wave broadcast service, I first analyze the available information from the Search Agent's retrieval.

The search query was focused on identifying the primary modulation mode used in short wave broadcast services. The retrieved results indicate:

- Doc 1 explicitly states that "the simplest type and the most commonly used for shortwave broadcasting" is amplitude modulation (AM), where the instantaneous amplitude of the carrier is controlled by the signal. It further notes that single sideband (SSB) is a form of amplitude modulation, which is also used in shortwave broadcasting, though it is more efficient in terms of power and bandwidth.

- Doc 2 mentions that frequency modulation (NBFM) is typically used above 20 MHz, but due to ionospheric distortions and bandwidth limitations in HF bands, it is not commonly used for long-distance broadcasting. It also notes that NBFM is limited to short-range transmissions.

- Doc 3 discusses other modes like CW (Morse code), radioteletype, and digital modes (e.g., DRM), but these are primarily used for data or specialized communications, not general broadcast services.

Given that shortwave broadcasting is primarily used for long-distance audio transmission (e.g., international radio broadcasts), and that AM is described as the most commonly used mode, even though SSB is more efficient, AM remains the foundational and historically dominant mode for shortwave broadcast services.

Therefore, despite the presence of SSB as a related modulation, the primary and most widely recognized mode for short wave broadcast service is amplitude modulation.

<answer>AM (Amplitude Modulation)</answer>

The instantaneous amplitude of the carrier is controlled by the amplitude of the signal (speech, or music, for example). At the receiver, a simple detector recovers the desired modulation signal from the carrier. Single sideband transmission is a form of amplitude modulation but in effect filters the result of modulation. An amplitude-modulated signal has frequency components both above and below the carrier frequency. If one set of these components is eliminated as well as the residual carrier, only the remaining set is transmitted. Regulations limit the bandwidth of a signal transmitted in the HF bands, and the advantages of frequency modulation are greatest if the FM signal has a wide bandwidth. NBFM is limited to short-range transmissions due to the multiphasic distortions created by the ionosphere. Digital Radio Mondiale (DRM) is a digital modulation for use on bands below 30 MHz. It is a digital signal, like the data modes, below, but is for transmitting audio, like the analog modes\nDoc 3: \"Shortwave radio\"\nabove. Continuous wave (CW) is on-and-off keying of a carrier, used for Morse code communications and Hellschreiber facsimile-based teleprinter transmissions. It is a data mode, although often listed separately. Radioteletype, fax, digital, slow-scan television, and other systems use forms of frequency-shift keying or audio subcarriers on a shortwave carrier. Note that on modern computer-driven systems, digital modes are typically sent by coupling a computer's sound output to the SSB input of a radio. answerer.response_text:
In 1...2...3... I reason that the question asks for the mode used in short wave broadcast service. Based on the retrieved information, shortwave broadcasting typically uses amplitude modulation (AM), which is described as the "simplest type and the most commonly used" for shortwave broadcasting. While single sideband (SSB) is mentioned as a form of amplitude modulation and is used in some shortwave transmissions, the primary and most common modulation mode for traditional shortwave broadcast services—especially for speech and music—is amplitude modulation (AM). Although frequency modulation (NBFM) and digital modes like DRM are used in specific contexts, AM remains the standard for broadcast services in the HF band.

Therefore, the primary modulation mode used in short wave broadcast service is amplitude modulation.

<answer>amplitude modulation</answer>
